question: My employer has not paid my holiday pay. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid you for holiday days you have taken, you may be entitled to receive holiday pay. Holiday pay is the money you should get for the time you take off work as annual leave. You should first check your contract and any company policies to understand your entitlement. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should raise the issue with your employer and ask them to pay what they owe you.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid holiday pay. It is important to keep any evidence that supports your claim, such as your contract, payslips, and records of the holiday you took.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain that you have not been paid for your holiday.  
Step 3: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 4: If still unresolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: How is holiday pay calculated if I work irregular hours?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your normal rate of pay, which includes commission, regular overtime payments, and other payments related to length of service or professional qualifications, but usually excludes bonuses. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at this normal rate of pay. To calculate your average pay, your employer will look at your pay and hours worked over the previous 52 weeks (or fewer if you have worked less time), averaging the pay for those weeks in which you were paid.

Employers cannot include holiday pay within your hourly rate (known as rolled-up holiday pay) for regular-hours workers, but rolled-up holiday pay may be used for irregular-hours and part-year workers unless your leave year began on or before 31 March 2024. If you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average weekly pay based on the last 52 weeks you worked and were paid.  
Step 2: They use this average to determine your holiday pay at your normal rate.  
Step 3: Your holiday pay is paid when you take your statutory leave.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer include holiday pay in my hourly rate?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include an amount for holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers, where rolled-up holiday pay can be used unless their leave year started on or before 31 March 2024. For most workers, holiday pay must be paid separately from the hourly wage.

If you work irregular hours or part of the year, your holiday pay is usually calculated based on the hours you have worked rather than being included in your hourly rate. For more detailed guidance or questions about your specific situation, you can contact ACAS.

Step 1: Check whether your work pattern is regular or irregular and when your leave year started.  
Step 2: Review your pay slips or contract to see if holiday pay is included in your hourly rate or paid separately.  
Step 3: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice if you believe your holiday pay is not being handled correctly.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: I have taken annual leave but did not get paid for it. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you have taken annual leave but were not paid for it, you are generally entitled to holiday pay for the time you took off. Your employer should pay you for any holiday days you have taken, as part of your statutory holiday entitlement. If your employer has not paid you for this leave, you should first check your employment contract and company policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay.

If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should gather any evidence such as your contract, payslips, and records of the leave you took. Then, raise the issue with your employer, providing this evidence and asking for the payment owed. If your employer refuses to pay, you may need to seek further advice or take formal steps to recover the money.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Collect evidence of your leave and unpaid holiday pay.  
Step 3: Contact your employer to request the payment.  
Step 4: If unpaid, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How much holiday am I legally entitled to in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year. This applies whether you work full-time, part-time, or under a zero-hours contract. For example, if you work five days a week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday annually, which can include bank holidays if your contract states so. If you work part-time, your holiday entitlement is calculated proportionally based on the number of days or hours you work.

If you have been employed for less than a full year, you are entitled to a proportion of the full holiday entitlement based on how long you have worked. Holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work and continues during periods such as probation, sick leave, or maternity/paternity leave. Some employers may offer enhanced holiday entitlement beyond the legal minimum, so it is important to check your contract for any additional holiday rights.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or written statement for your holiday entitlement details.  
Step 2: Calculate your entitlement based on your working pattern (full-time or part-time).  
Step 3: If unsure, speak to your employer, HR department, or contact the Acas helpline for clarification.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Does holiday continue to build up while I am off sick?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, holiday entitlement continues to build up while you are off work sick. Under UK employment law, workers have the right to accrue their statutory annual leave during periods of sickness absence. This means that even if you are not working due to illness, your holiday entitlement is still increasing as if you were working normally.

If you are unsure about how much holiday you have accrued or how to take it after a period of sickness, you can discuss this with your employer or HR department. You can also contact the Acas helpline for further guidance.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or speak with your employer about your holiday entitlement during sickness.  
Step 2: Keep records of your sickness absence and holiday accrued.  
Step 3: Contact Acas if you need more information or help resolving any disputes.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: What happens to my unused holiday when I leave my job?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave your job, you are entitled to be paid for any unused holiday you have accrued but not taken. This payment is usually included in your final pay. Your employer should calculate how much holiday pay you are owed based on your usual pay rate and the amount of holiday entitlement you have built up during your employment. The final pay may also include other elements, but holiday pay should be clearly identified on your payslip.

If you think your final pay does not include the correct amount for unused holiday, you should first speak to your employer to clarify how the final pay was calculated. If you do not receive your final payslip or have concerns about your final pay, you can contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Check your final payslip to see if unused holiday pay is included.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer if you believe the holiday pay is incorrect or missing.  
Step 3: Contact Acas for further help if you cannot resolve the issue with your employer.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Can my employer refuse my holiday request?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can refuse your holiday request, but they must have a good reason and should consider your request fairly. Workers have the right to request holiday, but employers can refuse if it would cause problems for the business, such as not having enough staff. It is best to make holiday requests in good time and follow your employer’s procedures.

If there is a dispute about holiday entitlement or pay, there are ways to resolve it, including discussing the issue with your employer or seeking advice from organisations like ACAS.

Step 1: Check your employer’s holiday request procedure and submit your request in good time.  
Step 2: If your request is refused, ask for the reason and see if you can agree on alternative dates.  
Step 3: If you believe your rights are not being met, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer force me to take holiday?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, your employer can require you to take holiday at a particular time, but they must give you notice that is at least twice as long as the amount of holiday they want you to take. For example, if your employer wants you to take one week of holiday, they must give you at least two weeks' notice. This is to ensure you have enough time to plan and prepare. Holiday entitlement is a legal right, and your employer must provide it, but they can decide when you take it within reason.

If you think your employer is not following the rules about holiday entitlement or holiday pay, you can raise the issue with them or seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your contract or company holiday policy for any specific rules about taking holiday.  
Step 2: If your employer wants you to take holiday, ensure they give you the correct notice (twice the holiday length).  
Step 3: If you disagree or have concerns, discuss this with your employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Do part-time workers get holiday pay?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, part-time workers in the UK are entitled to holiday pay. By law, all workers, including part-time employees, have the right to 5.6 weeks of statutory paid holiday per year. The amount of holiday pay for part-time workers is proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If an employer offers full-time workers more than the statutory minimum holiday entitlement, part-time workers should receive a proportionate increase as well.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of work and includes periods such as probation, sick leave, and statutory parental leave. If a part-time worker's contract lasts less than a year or ends partway through the holiday year, they are entitled to a proportion of the full year's holiday. Some contracts may also offer enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement beyond the statutory minimum.

Step 1: Check your employment contract to see your specific holiday entitlement and whether it includes any enhanced holiday pay.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday pay based on the hours you work, ensuring it aligns with the statutory minimum of 5.6 weeks per year.  
Step 3: If you believe your holiday pay is incorrect, you can seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer pay me instead of giving me holiday?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave. Generally, employers should allow you to take your holiday entitlement as time off rather than paying you instead. However, if you leave your job and have any unused holiday entitlement, your employer must pay you for this untaken holiday in your final pay. This payment is often called "holiday pay" and should be clearly shown on your payslip.

Employers cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, except in some cases for irregular-hours or part-year workers, and only if the leave year started before 31 March 2024. If you think your employer is not giving you your holiday entitlement or paying you correctly, you can contact ACAS for advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and holiday entitlement.
Step 2: Request to take your holiday as time off.
Step 3: If you leave your job with unused holiday, check your final pay to ensure you are paid for it.
Step 4: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice if you have concerns or disputes.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Is rolled-up holiday pay legal in the UK?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Rolled-up holiday pay, where holiday pay is included within the hourly rate rather than paid separately during holiday, is generally not allowed in the UK for most workers. However, there is an exception for irregular-hours and part-year workers, for whom rolled-up holiday pay can be used unless their leave year started on or before 31 March 2024. This means that for many workers, employers must pay holiday pay separately when the holiday is taken.

If you are unsure whether rolled-up holiday pay applies to your situation, you can contact the Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service (Acas) for guidance.

Step 1: Check if you are an irregular-hours or part-year worker.  
Step 2: Determine when your leave year began (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: Contact Acas if you need further clarification or believe your holiday pay is not being correctly paid.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: My employer has not paid my wages on payday. What should I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It might be a mistake they can quickly fix by paying you what you are owed straight away. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your case.

If the problem continues and your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try and resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: If no resolution, raise a formal grievance if possible.  
Step 3: Consider legal action through an employment tribunal or small claims court if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer deducted money from my wages without explaining why.

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for things like damaged property or missing money from the till, but only if there is a written agreement and certain rules are followed. These rules include not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, starting deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and giving you written details of the deduction each time money is taken.

If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements you have to see if such deductions are allowed. If you are unsure or believe the deduction is unfair, you should talk to an adviser for help.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t get a satisfactory answer, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I think I am being paid below minimum wage. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, you should first check the current rates for your age group or apprenticeship status on the official GOV.UK or ACAS websites. Employers are legally required to pay at least the minimum wage for the hours you work. If you find your pay is below the legal minimum, you should talk to your employer to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid to start tribunal proceedings.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age or apprenticeship status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence such as payslips and your contract.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer about the issue.  
Step 4: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 5: Consider legal action if the problem is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What can I do if my employer pays me late every month?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why your wages are delayed and explain that you expect to be paid on your agreed pay day. It’s important to keep a record of when you are paid and any conversations you have about the issue. If your employer has made a mistake, ask them to pay you as soon as possible and not to wait until the next pay day.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the problem and the late payments continue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the issue persists, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unauthorised deduction from wages. However, this should be a last resort, especially if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the late payments.  
Step 2: Keep records of payment dates and communications.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the problem continues.  
Step 4: Consider legal action, such as an employment tribunal claim, if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer make deductions from my wages?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only make deductions from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for things like damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform, but only if your contract says so or you have given written permission. There are extra rules if deductions are for missing money or damaged stock: the employer must have a written agreement, cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must give you written details of each deduction.

If you think your employer has made a deduction they shouldn’t have, you should check your contract and any agreements you have. If you are unsure or disagree with the deduction, you can talk to an adviser for help.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: If you disagree with a deduction, raise the issue with your employer and provide any evidence you have.  
Step 3: If the problem is not resolved, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer has not paid me for overtime. Is that legal?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your employment contract and the terms agreed upon. There is no automatic legal right to overtime pay unless your contract states it. However, your employer must pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked, including overtime. If your total pay divided by total hours worked (including overtime) falls below the minimum wage, your employer may be unlawfully underpaying you.

If you believe you have not been paid correctly for overtime, you should first check your contract and payslips to understand your entitlement. Then, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department. If this does not resolve the problem, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice and consider legal action within three months of the unpaid wages.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and payslips to confirm overtime pay terms.  
Step 2: Discuss the issue with your employer or payroll department.  
Step 3: If unresolved, get advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim for unpaid wages within the legal time limit.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What should be included on my payslip?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your payslip should clearly show details about your pay and any deductions made. This typically includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance contributions taken off, and any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments. It should also show your net pay (the amount you take home after deductions). Additionally, your payslip should include the pay period it covers and your employer’s name.

Payslips help you check that you are being paid correctly, including ensuring you receive at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for your age group. If you notice any discrepancies or deductions you do not agree with, you should first check your contract and any agreements you have with your employer. If you still have concerns, you can seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Review your payslip to ensure it includes all required information such as gross pay, deductions, and net pay.  
Step 2: Compare the payslip details with your employment contract and agreed pay.  
Step 3: If you find errors or unexplained deductions, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department.  
Step 4: If the problem is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/pay
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer withhold my final pay when I leave?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave a job, your employer must pay you your final pay, which may be different from your usual pay because it can include things like holiday pay, redundancy pay, or deductions for training courses. Your final pay should be given on your normal payday, even if you leave partway through a pay period. Employers should provide a payslip that clearly explains how your final pay was calculated.

If your employer withholds your final pay without a valid reason, you should first ask them for an explanation and a payslip showing the calculations. If you do not receive your final pay or payslip, you can contact the Acas helpline for guidance. If the issue is not resolved, you may be able to take further action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court, but it is advisable to seek advice before doing so.

Step 1: Ask your employer for your final payslip and an explanation of your final pay.  
Step 2: Contact the Acas helpline if you do not receive your final pay or if you have questions.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from Citizens Advice or a qualified adviser if the problem continues.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What can I do if my employer owes me commission?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer owes you commission, this is considered part of your pay and they should pay it according to the terms of your employment contract. If they haven’t paid what they owe, you should first try to talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing any evidence you have such as your contract or commission agreements. If this does not resolve the problem, check if your employer has a formal grievance procedure and consider raising a grievance to formally complain about the unpaid commission.

If your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from when the commission was due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It can be helpful to get advice from a trade union representative or an adviser before taking legal steps.

Step 1: Talk to your employer and show evidence of the commission owed.  
Step 2: Raise a formal grievance if talking does not work.  
Step 3: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Is it legal for my employer to charge me for training?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can require you to do training, but they cannot use mandatory training as a reason not to pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the time spent training. Even if your contract says you won’t be paid for mandatory training, your average hourly pay must still meet the legal minimum wage requirements. So, charging you for training or not paying you for mandatory training below the minimum wage is not lawful.

If you believe your employer is not paying you correctly for training time or is charging you for training unlawfully, you should gather any relevant documents such as your employment contract or company policies and seek advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any company policies about training and pay.  
Step 2: Confirm your entitlement to minimum wage for training hours.  
Step 3: If unpaid or charged unlawfully, contact HMRC or Citizens Advice for guidance on how to proceed.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: My employer says they overpaid me and wants the money back. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says they overpaid you and wants the money back, they can deduct the overpaid amount from your wages, but only if you agree in writing or if your contract allows it. If you don’t agree that you were overpaid, you should explain why and ask your employer to pay you what you believe you are owed. If you were overpaid but cannot afford to repay the full amount immediately, you can ask your employer if they will accept smaller deductions spread over a longer period.

Your employer must follow certain rules if they deduct money for overpayments. For example, deductions should not take your pay below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage. Also, if deductions are made for missing money or damaged stock, they must have a written agreement, not deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax each payday, start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and provide you with written details of each deduction.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements to see if your employer can deduct overpayments.  
Step 2: If you disagree with the overpayment, explain your reasons to your employer and ask for the correct pay.  
Step 3: If you agree you were overpaid but cannot repay immediately, ask if smaller deductions over time are possible.  
Step 4: If you think deductions are unlawful or too high, seek advice from an adviser or a trade union representative.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Can my employer change my pay without telling me?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should not change your pay without informing you. Any changes to your pay should be agreed upon, usually in writing, as part of your contract or through a formal variation to your contract. If your employer deducts money from your wages or changes your pay, they must have your agreement or a clear contractual right to do so. If you notice a deduction or change you don’t agree with, you should check your contract and speak to your employer to understand why the change was made.

If you believe your pay has been unlawfully reduced or deductions made without your consent, you can raise the issue with your employer first. If this does not resolve the problem, you may seek advice or take further action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court within the relevant time limits.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for terms about pay changes or deductions.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer or payroll department to clarify the change.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser.  
Step 4: Consider formal action if necessary, but be aware of time limits for claims.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What should I do if I have not been paid for work I have already done?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why you haven’t been paid. Check your payslip if you have one to understand the problem better. If it was a mistake, ask your employer to pay you straight away and not wait until your next pay day. If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. The deadline to start this process is usually 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It’s important to keep any evidence like contracts, payslips, or written agreements to support your claim.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to clarify the issue.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 3: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim or small claims court if necessary, keeping in mind the time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I was dismissed without a meeting. Is that unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a sign that your employer did not follow a fair procedure. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer should have a valid reason and act reasonably, which usually includes investigating the situation and giving you a chance to respond, often through a meeting. If you were dismissed without any meeting or opportunity to explain your side, this could mean the dismissal was unfair, especially if you have been employed for at least two years.

You have the right to ask your employer for a written statement explaining why you were dismissed. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. However, it is important to follow the correct procedures and time limits.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s internal process.  
Step 3: If you still believe the dismissal was unfair, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me and refused to let me appeal.
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to let you appeal, this may be unfair, especially if they did not follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Employers are generally expected to allow employees the right to appeal a dismissal decision as part of a fair process. Not allowing an appeal could be seen as failing to follow a fair procedure, which is important if you want to make a claim for unfair dismissal.

You should ask your employer in writing why you were dismissed and whether you can appeal the decision. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement of reasons for your dismissal within 14 days. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal, but it is usually best to try to resolve the issue with your employer first.

Step 1: Request a written explanation for your dismissal and ask about the appeal process.  
Step 2: Check your employment status and length of service to understand your rights.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options, including making an unfair dismissal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be dismissed without notice?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, you can be dismissed without notice in certain situations, such as if you are dismissed for gross misconduct (for example, violence). This is known as summary dismissal. However, in most cases, your employer must give you at least the notice period stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. Your employer must have a valid reason for dismissal, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure.

If you are dismissed, your employer should tell you why you have been dismissed, when your employment ends, and your right to appeal. You have the right to ask for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012). Pregnant employees and those on maternity leave have additional rights to receive this information regardless of length of service.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your notice period.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for a written statement of reasons if you have been employed long enough.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, consider appealing or seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What is constructive dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that fundamentally breaches their employment contract. This could include things like a significant change to the employee’s job without agreement, not paying them, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must feel they have no choice but to leave because of the employer’s conduct.

For a claim of constructive dismissal to be considered, the employee usually needs to have been employed for at least two years. It is important that the employee follows a fair procedure, and if they believe they have been constructively dismissed, they may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Keep a record of the employer’s behaviour that you believe breaches your contract.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, if possible, to try to resolve the problem.  
Step 3: If you decide to resign, seek advice before doing so to understand your rights and the process for making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: How long do I need to work somewhere to claim unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

To claim unfair dismissal in the UK, you generally need to have worked for your employer for at least 2 years. This means you must have the legal status of an employee and have been continuously employed for that period. If you have worked for less than 2 years, you usually cannot claim unfair dismissal, except in certain cases such as dismissal related to pregnancy or whistleblowing.

If you believe you have been unfairly dismissed, you should first appeal the dismissal with your employer following a fair procedure. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Keep in mind that the employer must provide reasons for dismissal in writing if you have been employed for 2 years or more and request it.

Step 1: Check your length of continuous employment (at least 2 years needed).
Step 2: Appeal the dismissal with your employer, following a fair procedure.
Step 3: If unsuccessful, consider making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer says I was dismissed for poor performance. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says you were dismissed for poor performance, they must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. This means they should have investigated the situation fully and followed a fair procedure before dismissing you. Your employer should be consistent and not dismiss you for something they have allowed other employees to do. You have the right to be told why you were dismissed, when your employment ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal the decision. It is best if this is provided in writing.

If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer and, if necessary, make a claim to an employment tribunal. However, if you accept a settlement agreement, you usually cannot make an unfair dismissal claim.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have the required length of service.  
Step 2: Consider whether your employer followed a fair procedure and acted reasonably.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal was unfair, appeal the decision with your employer.  
Step 4: If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may consider making a claim to an employment tribunal (within the time limits).

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be dismissed while on sick leave?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can be dismissed while on sick leave, but your employer must have a fair and valid reason for doing so and must act reasonably in the circumstances. They should have investigated the situation fully before dismissing you. For example, if your absence is due to long-term sickness, your employer may consider dismissal if they believe you cannot return to work in a reasonable time or if they have explored reasonable adjustments. However, the dismissal must not be discriminatory or unfair.

If you are dismissed while on Statutory Maternity Leave, your employer must give you a written statement explaining the reasons for dismissal regardless of how long you have worked for them. For other types of sick leave, you have the right to request a written statement of reasons if you have at least two years’ continuous service (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012).

Step 1: Check your employment contract and company policies on sick leave and dismissal.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for a written explanation if you are dismissed while on sick leave.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair or discriminatory, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What should happen in a fair disciplinary process?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A fair disciplinary process in the workplace should follow a clear, reasonable, and consistent procedure. The employer must have a valid reason for any disciplinary action and should investigate the matter thoroughly before making a decision. The employee should be informed of the issues and given a chance to respond to any allegations against them. The process should be conducted in line with the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures, which helps ensure fairness.

The employer should explain the reasons for any disciplinary action or dismissal clearly and, if dismissal occurs, provide the employee with the reasons in writing. The employee should also be given the right to appeal the decision. Confidentiality should be maintained throughout the process to protect the privacy of all involved.

Step 1: Employer investigates the issue and gathers evidence.  
Step 2: Employee is informed of the concerns and invited to a disciplinary meeting.  
Step 3: Employee is given a chance to explain their side.  
Step 4: Employer makes a balanced and fair decision.  
Step 5: Employer communicates the decision and reasons in writing.  
Step 6: Employee is given the right to appeal the decision.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be made redundant without consultation?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers are generally expected to consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is an important part of the redundancy process and should involve discussing the reasons for redundancy, exploring alternatives, and considering ways to avoid or reduce redundancies. This applies whether the redundancy affects one person or a group of employees.

If an employer fails to consult properly, the redundancy may be considered unfair. However, there are some exceptions, such as in very small businesses or in cases of genuine emergency where consultation may not be possible. It is advisable to check the specific circumstances and seek guidance if you believe consultation has not been carried out.

Step 1: Ask your employer for details about the redundancy process and whether consultation has taken place.  
Step 2: If you feel consultation was inadequate, you can seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 3: Consider whether you may have grounds to challenge the redundancy as unfair.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What is the difference between redundancy and dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which is often called being sacked or fired. It can happen for various reasons, such as poor performance, misconduct, or redundancy. Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that happens when an employer needs to reduce their workforce because the job you do is no longer needed, for example, due to business closure or changes in work requirements.

The key difference is that redundancy is related to the role being eliminated, not the employee's conduct or performance. Both redundancy and other dismissals require the employer to follow a fair and reasonable procedure, including giving valid reasons and notice where applicable.

Step 1: Understand the reason for the dismissal—whether it is due to redundancy or another cause.  
Step 2: Check if your employer has followed a fair procedure and given proper notice.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal or seek advice from ACAS or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer dismissed me for raising a complaint. Is this unfair?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and related to a workplace issue. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Dismissing someone for raising a complaint might be considered a breach of this, particularly if it relates to whistleblowing or raising concerns about wrongdoing.

You have the right to appeal against the dismissal and your employer should follow a fair procedure during this process. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act promptly, as there are time limits for making such claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 2: Use the appeal process if you want to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal if you remain dissatisfied.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can I challenge my dismissal at a tribunal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure, including investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been dismissed, you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, especially if you have been employed for at least two years or are on statutory maternity leave.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally with your employer first. If that does not resolve the issue, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Keep in mind that the employer should follow a fair and reasonable procedure, and failure to do so can strengthen your claim. However, if you settle the matter through a settlement agreement, you usually cannot make an unfair dismissal claim.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s internal procedures.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may bring a claim to an employment tribunal, but be aware of strict time limits for doing so.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice to understand your rights and the process.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I believe my dismissal was unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you should first check whether your employer followed a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing you. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and act consistently and fairly. You have the right to appeal the dismissal with your employer, and they should follow a fair process during this appeal. If your appeal is successful and you get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," meaning you cannot then claim unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal.

If you remain unhappy after the appeal or your employer did not follow a fair procedure, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. It is important to act promptly, as there are strict time limits for making claims. You can also seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice to understand your options and the best steps to take.

Step 1: Ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you have not already received them.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on making an employment tribunal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: I was treated unfairly at work because of my religion. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim. Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination and should take any complaints seriously.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly at work because of your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure. Employers should follow a fair process to address your concerns. If the issue is not resolved, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to keep records of any incidents or communications related to the discrimination.

Step 1: Document the incidents of discrimination, including dates, times, and what happened.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through their grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: My manager is harassing me. Is this discrimination or harassment?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, or sexual orientation. It can include offensive jokes, insults, or other behaviour that creates a hostile or intimidating work environment. If your manager's behaviour is unwanted and related to one of these protected characteristics, it may be considered harassment.

Discrimination involves treating someone unfairly because of a protected characteristic. Harassment is a form of discrimination but specifically focuses on unwanted conduct related to those characteristics. If your manager's behaviour is not linked to a protected characteristic but is still unwanted and upsetting, it may be bullying rather than harassment.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, including dates, times, and what was said or done.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer following their complaints procedure or speak to HR.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you can seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What is workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. Protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly, indirectly, or through harassment or victimisation.

The law protects employees and workers from discrimination at work, and employers must not discriminate against anyone with these protected characteristics. This protection also extends to people associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or those who have complained about discrimination or supported a claim.

Step 1: If you believe you are being discriminated against at work, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal complaint procedure. Step 2: Employers are required to take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and bullying. Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking further advice or making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be treated differently because of my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal to treat someone unfairly or differently because of a disability. Disability is one of the protected characteristics, meaning you are protected from discrimination at work and in other areas such as education and when using public services. Employers must not discriminate against you because of your disability, and they should make reasonable adjustments to help you do your job.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your disability, you can raise the issue with your employer or seek advice on how to handle discrimination. Employers are also encouraged to take positive action to support people with disabilities if they face disadvantages or are under-represented.

Step 1: Understand your rights under the Equality Act 2010 regarding disability discrimination.  
Step 2: If you experience discrimination, consider raising the issue informally with your employer or through a formal complaint procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice if you need further support or want to understand your options.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What is victimisation at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats you badly because you have made a complaint about discrimination, supported someone else’s discrimination claim, or raised a concern about discrimination. It is a form of unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include actions like being unfairly disciplined, denied promotion, or subjected to bullying because of your involvement in a discrimination issue.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and to take any complaints seriously. If you believe you are being victimised at work, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. You may also seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents that you believe are victimisation.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, following their grievance procedure if they have one.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor if the problem continues.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: My employer ignored my complaint about discrimination. What can I do?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer ignores your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue through your employer’s formal grievance procedure if they have one. Employers are legally required to take discrimination complaints seriously and to take steps to prevent discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. If the employer fails to address your complaint, you may consider seeking advice from external organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

You can also consider making a claim to an employment tribunal for discrimination if the issue is not resolved internally. It is important to keep records of your complaint and any responses or lack thereof from your employer. Remember, you are protected from discrimination because of protected characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, and others under the Equality Act 2010.

Step 1: Raise your complaint formally with your employer using their grievance procedure.  
Step 2: Keep a record of all communications and responses.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for support and guidance.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Is bullying at work illegal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Bullying at work is not a specific criminal offence, but it is considered unacceptable behaviour and can be related to unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 if it is connected to protected characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, or others. Employers have a legal duty to prevent bullying and harassment and to take complaints seriously. If bullying amounts to discrimination, harassment, or victimisation as defined by the Equality Act, it can be unlawful.

If you experience or witness bullying at work, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should have policies and procedures in place to handle bullying and discrimination complaints and are responsible for creating a safe working environment.

Step 1: Report the bullying to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 2: Keep a record of incidents, including dates, times, and witnesses.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about further steps, including potential legal claims.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What counts as harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. Protected characteristics include age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, sexual orientation, pregnancy and maternity, marriage and civil partnership. Harassment can be verbal, physical, or written, and may include jokes, insults, bullying, or offensive gestures related to these characteristics.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent harassment and to take complaints seriously. If you experience harassment, you can raise it informally or formally with your employer, who should investigate and address the issue. Witnesses to harassment can also report it to help stop it.

Step 1: Identify if the behaviour is linked to a protected characteristic and if it is unwanted or offensive.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or formally.  
Step 3: If the employer does not take appropriate action, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Can my employer refuse reasonable adjustments for my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, employers have a duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities to help them overcome disadvantages caused by their disability at work. This means your employer should consider making changes to your working environment, job duties, or workplace policies if these changes would help you perform your job better. However, the adjustments must be "reasonable," which generally means they should not cause the employer disproportionate difficulty or expense.

If your employer refuses to make reasonable adjustments without a valid reason, this could be considered discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. You can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal grievance procedure if available. If the problem persists, you may seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Inform your employer about your disability and the adjustments you believe you need.  
Step 2: Provide any medical evidence or documentation if requested.  
Step 3: Discuss possible reasonable adjustments with your employer.  
Step 4: If your employer refuses, ask for a clear explanation.  
Step 5: Consider raising a formal grievance or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: I was treated unfairly because of my race. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, it is illegal to be treated unfairly or discriminated against because of your race, which is a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010. This protection applies at work, meaning your employer must not discriminate against you in hiring, pay, promotion, or dismissal, and must prevent harassment or victimisation related to your race. If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your race, you can raise the issue with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and follow a fair process to address it.

You can choose to take informal or formal steps to resolve the matter, such as discussing it with your employer or making a formal complaint. If the issue is not resolved, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Employers are also encouraged to take positive action to support people from under-represented racial groups.

Step 1: Document the incidents where you believe you were treated unfairly because of your race.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, you should not be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint. If you raise a complaint about discrimination under the Equality Act 2010, your employer must not treat you unfairly or dismiss you because of that complaint. Dismissing someone for making a discrimination complaint could be considered unfair dismissal or victimisation, which is unlawful.

If you believe you have been dismissed because you made a discrimination complaint, you may have grounds to challenge the dismissal as unfair. Your employer must have a valid reason for dismissal and follow a fair procedure. If you have been dismissed, you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, especially if you have worked for your employer for at least two years.

Step 1: Keep records of your complaint and any related communications.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your situation and possible next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What should I do if I experience harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you experience harassment at work, your employer has a legal duty to prevent it and take your complaint seriously. You can choose to address the issue informally by speaking to your employer or HR department, or formally by making a written complaint through your employer’s grievance procedure. Employers should investigate harassment complaints promptly and confidentially to resolve the situation.

It is important to keep a record of what happened, including dates, times, locations, and any witnesses, as this can support your complaint. If you feel unable to raise the issue with your employer or are not satisfied with their response, you may seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Keep a detailed record of the harassment incidents.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or formally through their grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
